revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: ''''みあれ祭'''（みあれさい）は、毎年10月1日に[[福岡県]][[宗像市]]の[[宗像大社]]秋季大祭で海上安全や大漁など願って行…'
revision.text:
'''みあれ祭'''（みあれさい）は、毎年10月1日に[[福岡県]][[宗像市]]の[[宗像大社]]秋季大祭で海上安全や大漁など願って行われる祭礼。[[漁船]]およそ数百隻による大規模な海上神幸行事で知られる。
== 概要 == 宗像大社は本土にある'''辺津宮'''、[[大島]]にある'''中津宮'''、[[沖ノ島]]にある'''沖津宮'''からなり、それぞれ姉妹神である[[宗像三女神]]が祀られている。みあれ祭は辺津宮に祀られている市杵島姫神（いちきしまひめ） が、姉にあたる田心姫神（たごりひめ）と湍津姫神（たぎつひめ）を迎えるもので、海上では宗像三女神それぞれの御輿を載せた3隻の御座船を中心に大規模な船団が周回する。
なお、みあれ祭の空撮映像は[[NHK福岡放送局]]制作の[[2003年]]の[[福岡発地域ドラマ]]「玄海〜わたしの海へ〜」のエンディングで用いられた。また、みあれ祭は[[2005年]]に[[RKB毎日放送]]制作の『[[ダイドードリンコ]]スペシャル 海と女神の物語 みあれ祭』で紹介された。
{{DEFAULTSORT:みあれさい}} [[Category:福岡県の祭り]] [[Category:宗像市]] {{event-stub}}

'''みあれ祭'''（みあれさい）は、毎年10月1日に[[福岡県]][[宗像市]]の[[宗像大社]]秋季大祭で海上安全や大漁など願って行われる祭礼。[[漁船]]およそ数百隻による大規模な海上神幸行事で知られる。
== 概要 == 宗像大社は本土にある'''辺津宮'''、[[大島 (福岡県)|大島]]にある'''中津宮'''、[[沖ノ島]]にある'''沖津宮'''からなり、それぞれ姉妹神である[[宗像三女神]]が祀られている。みあれ祭は辺津宮に祀られている市杵島姫神（いちきしまひめ） が、姉にあたる田心姫神（たごりひめ）と湍津姫神（たぎつひめ）を迎えるもので、海上では宗像三女神それぞれの御輿を載せた3隻の御座船を中心に大規模な船団が周回する。
なお、みあれ祭の空撮映像は[[NHK福岡放送局]]制作の[[2003年]]の[[福岡発地域ドラマ]]「玄海〜わたしの海へ〜」のエンディングで用いられた。また、みあれ祭は[[2005年]]に[[RKB毎日放送]]制作の『[[ダイドードリンコ]]スペシャル 海と女神の物語 みあれ祭』で紹介された。
{{DEFAULTSORT:みあれさい}} [[Category:福岡県の祭り]] [[Category:宗像市]] {{event-stub}}

'''みあれ祭'''（みあれさい）は、毎年[[10月1日]]に[[福岡県]][[宗像市]]の[[宗像大社]]秋季大祭で海上安全や大漁など願って行われる祭礼である。[[漁船]]およそ数百隻による大規模な海上神幸行事で知られる。
== 内容 == 宗像大社は本土にある'''辺津宮'''、[[大島 (福岡県)|大島]]にある'''中津宮'''、[[沖ノ島]]にある'''沖津宮'''からなり、それぞれ姉妹神である[[宗像三女神]]が祀られている。みあれ祭は辺津宮に祀られている市杵島姫神（いちきしまひめ） が、姉にあたる田心姫神（たごりひめ）と湍津姫神（たぎつひめ）を迎えるもので、海上では宗像三女神それぞれの御輿を載せた3隻の御座船を中心に大規模な船団が周回する。
== メディアへの登場 == みあれ祭の空撮映像は[[NHK福岡放送局]]制作の[[2003年]]の[[福岡発地域ドラマ]]「玄海〜わたしの海へ〜」のエンディングで用いられた。
みあれ祭は[[2005年]]に[[RKB毎日放送]]制作の『[[ダイドードリンコ]]スペシャル 海と女神の物語 みあれ祭』で紹介された。
{{event-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:みあれさい}} [[Category:福岡県の祭り]] [[Category:宗像市]] [[Category:10月]]

{{出典の明記|date=2014年3月29日 (土) 09:58 (UTC)}} {{画像提供依頼|date=2014年3月29日 (土) 09:58 (UTC)|cat=福岡県|祭りの様子がわかる画像}} '''みあれ祭'''（みあれさい）は、毎年[[10月1日]]に[[福岡県]][[宗像市]]の[[宗像大社]]秋季大祭で海上安全や大漁など願って行われる祭礼である。[[漁船]]およそ数百隻による大規模な海上神幸行事で知られる。宗像氏は海人（あま）族として知られ、優れた航海技術をもち、日本と大陸との交易に重要な役割を果たしてきた。
== 内容 == 宗像大社は本土にある'''辺津宮'''、[[大島 (福岡県)|大島]]にある'''中津宮'''、[[沖ノ島]]にある'''沖津宮'''からなり、それぞれ姉妹神である[[宗像三女神]]が祀られている。みあれ祭は辺津宮に祀られている市杵島姫神（いちきしまひめ） が、姉にあたる田心姫神（たごりひめ）と湍津姫神（たぎつひめ）を迎えるもので、海上では宗像三女神それぞれの御輿を載せた3隻の御座船（ござぶね）が宗像七浦（ななうら）の船団に供奉（ぐぶ）されて、海上を巡行する。
== メディアへの登場 == みあれ祭の空撮映像は[[NHK福岡放送局]]制作の[[2003年]]の[[福岡発地域ドラマ]]「玄海〜わたしの海へ〜」のエンディングで用いられた。
みあれ祭は[[2005年]]に[[RKB毎日放送]]制作の『[[ダイドードリンコ]]スペシャル 海と女神の物語 みあれ祭』で紹介された。
==参考文献== * 福岡市 編『ふくおか歴史散歩』（[[平野國臣]]が宗像大社の営繕を担当） * [[藤原新也]]・[[安部龍太郎]]『神の島 沖ノ島』（[[小学館]] 2013年）
{{event-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:みあれさい}} [[Category:福岡県の祭り]] [[Category:宗像市]] [[Category:10月]]

== メディアへの登場 == みあれ祭の空撮映像は[[NHK福岡放送局]]制作の[[2003年]]の[[福岡発地域ドラマ]]「玄海〜わたしの海へ〜」のエンディングで用いられた。
みあれ祭は[[2005年]]に[[RKB毎日放送]]制作の『[[ダイドードリンコ]]スペシャル 海と女神の物語 みあれ祭』で紹介された。
==参考文献== * 福岡市 編『ふくおか歴史散歩』（[[平野國臣]]が宗像大社の営繕を担当） * [[藤原新也]]・[[安部龍太郎]]『神の島 沖ノ島』（[[小学館]] 2013年）

{{出典の明記|date=2014年3月29日 (土) 09:58 (UTC)}} {{画像提供依頼|date=2014年3月29日 (土) 09:58 (UTC)|cat=宗像市|祭りの様子がわかる画像}} '''みあれ祭'''（みあれさい）は、毎年[[10月1日]]に[[福岡県]][[宗像市]]の[[宗像大社]]秋季大祭（田島放生会）の最初に行われる海上、航海安全や大漁などを願って行われる祭礼である。
== 内容 == 宗像大社は本土にある'''辺津宮'''、[[大島 (福岡県)|大島]]にある'''中津宮'''、[[沖ノ島]]にある'''沖津宮'''からなり、それぞれ姉妹神である[[宗像三女神]]が祀られている。みあれ祭は辺津宮に祀られている市杵島姫神（いちきしまひめ） が、姉にあたる田心姫神（たごりひめ）と湍津姫神（たぎつひめ）を迎えるもので、海上では宗像三女神それぞれの御輿を載せた3隻の御座船（ござぶね）が宗像七浦（ななうら）の船団に供奉（ぐぶ）されて、海上を巡行する。[[漁船]]およそ数百隻による大規模な海上神幸行事で知られる。[[宗像氏]]は有史以前の上代、古代から[[海人族]](あまぞく)として知られ、優れた航海技術をもち、日本と大陸との交易に重要な役割を果たしてきたとされる。

== 内容 == 宗像大社は本土にある'''辺津宮'''、[[大島 (福岡県)|大島]]にある'''中津宮'''、[[沖ノ島]]にある'''沖津宮'''からなり、それぞれ姉妹神である[[宗像三女神]]が祀られている。みあれ祭は辺津宮に祀られている市杵島姫神（いちきしまひめ） が、姉にあたる田心姫神（たごりひめ）と湍津姫神（たぎつひめ）を迎えるもので、海上では宗像三女神それぞれの御輿を載せた3隻の御座船（ござぶね）が[[宗像七浦]]（ななうら）の船団に供奉（ぐぶ）されて、海上を巡行する。[[漁船]]およそ数百隻による大規模な海上神幸行事で知られる。[[宗像氏]]は有史以前の上代、古代から[[海人族]](あまぞく)として知られ、優れた航海技術をもち、日本と大陸との交易に重要な役割を果たしてきたとされる。
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|海上神幸行事のシーン（2014年）]]
== メディアへの登場 == [[NHK福岡放送局]]制作の[[2003年]]の[[福岡発地域ドラマ]]「玄海〜わたしの海へ〜」のエンディングで用いられた。 [[2005年]]に[[RKB毎日放送]]制作の『[[ダイドードリンコ]]スペシャル 海と女神の物語 みあれ祭』で紹介された。
== 関連項目 == * [[宗像大社]] * [[船渡御]] * [[放生会]] * [[海人族]]
== 参考文献 == * 福岡市 編『ふくおか歴史散歩』（[[平野國臣]]が宗像大社の営繕を担当） * [[藤原新也]]・[[安部龍太郎]]『神の島 沖ノ島』（[[小学館]] 2013年） *[https://profile.ameba.jp/ameba/toyuden/ 堀田はりい]『神宿る沖ノ島－古代日本人が見えてくる』第7章【女人禁制考とみあれ祭】「御長手神事とみあれ祭」（[http://www.yubun-shoin.co.jp/book/book_detail/4-8421-0789-9.html 右文書院]、2018年）
{{event-stub}}

The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful vo..."

revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}} as part of the {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in {{ill|Munakata City|ja|宗像市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島}}, each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御}} * {{ill|Hōjō-e|ja|放生会}} * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * {{ill|Shinya Fujiwara|ja|藤原新也}} and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.
{{event-stub}}
[[Category:Festivals in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Munakata City]] [[Category:October]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[ja:みあれ祭]]

During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.

{{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}} as part of the {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in {{ill|Munakata City|ja|宗像市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島}}, each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御}} * {{ill|Hōjō-e|ja|放生会}} * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * {{ill|Shinya Fujiwara|ja|藤原新也}} and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.
{{event-stub}}

During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). {{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}} as part of the {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in {{ill|Munakata City|ja|宗像市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島}}, each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御}} * {{ill|Hōjō-e|ja|放生会}} * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * {{ill|Shinya Fujiwara|ja|藤原新也}} and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.
{{event-stub}}

In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.

{{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}} as part of the {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in {{ill|Munakata City|ja|宗像市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島}}, each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御}} * {{ill|Hōjō-e|ja|放生会}} * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * {{ill|Shinya Fujiwara|ja|藤原新也}} and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.
{{event-stub}}

{{出典の明記|date=2014年3月29日 (土) 09:58 (UTC)}} {{画像提供依頼|date=2014年3月29日 (土) 09:58 (UTC)|cat=宗像市|祭りの様子がわかる画像}} '''みあれ祭'''（みあれさい）は、毎年[[10月1日]]に[[福岡県]][[宗像市]]の[[宗像大社]]秋季大祭（田島放生会）の最初に行われる海上、航海安全や大漁などを願って行われる祭礼である。
== 内容 == 宗像大社は本土にある'''辺津宮'''、[[大島 (福岡県)|大島]]にある'''中津宮'''、[[沖ノ島]]にある'''沖津宮'''からなり、それぞれ姉妹神である[[宗像三女神]]が祀られている。みあれ祭は辺津宮に祀られている市杵島姫神（いちきしまひめ） が、姉にあたる田心姫神（たごりひめ）と湍津姫神（たぎつひめ）を迎えるもので、海上では宗像三女神それぞれの御輿を載せた3隻の御座船（ござぶね）が宗像七浦（ななうら）の船団に供奉（ぐぶ）されて、海上を巡行する。[[漁船]]およそ数百隻による大規模な海上神幸行事で知られる。[[宗像氏]]は有史以前の上代、古代から[[海人族]](あまぞく)として知られ、優れた航海技術をもち、日本と大陸との交易に重要な役割を果たしてきたとされる。
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|海上神幸行事のシーン（2014年）]]
== メディアへの登場 == [[NHK福岡放送局]]制作の[[2003年]]の[[福岡発地域ドラマ]]「玄海〜わたしの海へ〜」のエンディングで用いられた。 [[2005年]]に[[RKB毎日放送]]制作の『[[ダイドードリンコ]]スペシャル 海と女神の物語 みあれ祭』で紹介された。
== 関連項目 == * [[宗像大社]] * [[船渡御]] * [[放生会]] * [[海人族]]
== 参考文献 == * 福岡市 編『ふくおか歴史散歩』（[[平野国臣]]が宗像大社の営繕を担当） * [[藤原新也]]・[[安部龍太郎]]『神の島 沖ノ島』（[[小学館]] 2013年）
{{event-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:みあれさい}} [[Category:福岡県の祭り]] [[Category:宗像市]] [[Category:10月]]

{{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}} as part of the {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in {{ill|Munakata City|ja|宗像市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島}}, each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御}} * {{ill|Hōjō-e|ja|放生会}} * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * {{ill|Shinya Fujiwara|ja|藤原新也}} and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.
{{event-stub}}

{{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}} as part of the {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in {{ill|Munakata City|ja|宗像市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島}}, each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御}} * {{ill|Hōjō-e|ja|放生会}} * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * {{ill|Shinya Fujiwara|ja|藤原新也}} and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.
{{event-stub}}

{{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}} as part of the {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in {{ill|Munakata City|ja|宗像市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島}}, each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御}} * {{ill|Hōjō-e|ja|放生会}} * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * {{ill|Shinya Fujiwara|ja|藤原新也}} and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.
{{event-stub}}

{{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}} as part of the {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in {{ill|Munakata City|ja|宗像市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島}}, each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御}} * {{ill|Hōjō-e|ja|放生会}} * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * {{ill|Shinya Fujiwara|ja|藤原新也}} and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.
{{event-stub}}

{{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}} as part of the {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in {{ill|Munakata City|ja|宗像市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島}}, each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御}} * {{ill|Hōjō-e|ja|放生会}} * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * {{ill|Shinya Fujiwara|ja|藤原新也}} and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.
{{event-stub}}

{{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}} as part of the {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in {{ill|Munakata City|ja|宗像市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島}}, each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御}} * {{ill|Hōjō-e|ja|放生会}} * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * {{ill|Shinya Fujiwara|ja|藤原新也}} and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.
{{event-stub}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "宗像三女神}}" to "宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}"
revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}} as part of the {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in {{ill|Munakata City|ja|宗像市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島}}, each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御}} * {{ill|Hōjō-e|ja|放生会}} * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * {{ill|Shinya Fujiwara|ja|藤原新也}} and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.
{{event-stub}}

{{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}} as part of the {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in {{ill|Munakata City|ja|宗像市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島}}, each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御}} * {{ill|Hōjō-e|ja|放生会}} * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * {{ill|Shinya Fujiwara|ja|藤原新也}} and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.
{{event-stub}}

In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.

{{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}} as part of the {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in {{ill|Munakata City|ja|宗像市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島}}, each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御}} * {{ill|Hōjō-e|ja|放生会}} * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * {{ill|Shinya Fujiwara|ja|藤原新也}} and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.

The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.

{{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}} as part of the {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in {{ill|Munakata City|ja|宗像市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島}}, each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御}} * {{ill|Hōjō-e|ja|放生会}} * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * {{ill|Shinya Fujiwara|ja|藤原新也}} and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.
{{event-stub}}

{{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}} as part of the {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in {{ill|Munakata City|ja|宗像市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御}} * {{ill|Hōjō-e|ja|放生会}} * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * {{ill|Shinya Fujiwara|ja|藤原新也}} and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.

{{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}} as part of the {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in {{ill|Munakata City|ja|宗像市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島}}, each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御}} * {{ill|Hōjō-e|ja|放生会}} * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * {{ill|Shinya Fujiwara|ja|藤原新也}} and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.
{{event-stub}}

{{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}} as part of the {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in {{ill|Munakata City|ja|宗像市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島}}, each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御}} * {{ill|Hōjō-e|ja|放生会}} * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * {{ill|Shinya Fujiwara|ja|藤原新也}} and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.
{{event-stub}}

{{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}} as part of the {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in {{ill|Munakata City|ja|宗像市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島}}, each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御}} * {{ill|Hōjō-e|ja|放生会}} * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * {{ill|Shinya Fujiwara|ja|藤原新也}} and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.

{{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}} as part of the {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in {{ill|Munakata City|ja|宗像市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島}}, each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御}} * {{ill|Hōjō-e|ja|放生会}} * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * {{ill|Shinya Fujiwara|ja|藤原新也}} and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.

{{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}} as part of the {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in {{ill|Munakata City|ja|宗像市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島}}, each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.

revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}} as part of the {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in {{ill|Munakata City|ja|宗像市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島}}, each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御}} * {{ill|Hōjō-e|ja|放生会}} * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * {{ill|Shinya Fujiwara|ja|藤原新也}} and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.
{{event-stub}}

{{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}} as part of the {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in {{ill|Munakata City|ja|宗像市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.

During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.

== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島}}, each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御}} * {{ill|Hōjō-e|ja|放生会}} * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * {{ill|Shinya Fujiwara|ja|藤原新也}} and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.

{{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}} as part of the {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in {{ill|Munakata City|ja|宗像市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島}}, each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御}} * {{ill|Hōjō-e|ja|放生会}} * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * {{ill|Shinya Fujiwara|ja|藤原新也}} and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.

{{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}} as part of the {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in {{ill|Munakata City|ja|宗像市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島}}, each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.

{{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}} as part of the {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in {{ill|Munakata City|ja|宗像市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島}}, each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御}} * {{ill|Hōjō-e|ja|放生会}} * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * {{ill|Shinya Fujiwara|ja|藤原新也}} and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.

{{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}} as part of the {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in {{ill|Munakata City|ja|宗像市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島}}, each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.

{{出典の明記|date=2014年3月29日 (土) 09:58 (UTC)}} {{画像提供依頼|date=2014年3月29日 (土) 09:58 (UTC)|cat=宗像市|祭りの様子がわかる画像}} '''みあれ祭'''（みあれさい）は、毎年[[10月1日]]に[[福岡県]][[宗像市]]の[[宗像大社]]秋季大祭（田島放生会）の最初に行われる海上、航海安全や大漁などを願って行われる祭礼である。
== 内容 == 宗像大社は本土にある'''辺津宮'''、[[大島 (福岡県)|大島]]にある'''中津宮'''、[[沖ノ島]]にある'''沖津宮'''からなり、それぞれ姉妹神である[[宗像三女神]]が祀られている。みあれ祭は辺津宮に祀られている市杵島姫神（いちきしまひめ）が、姉にあたる田心姫神（たごりひめ）と湍津姫神（たぎつひめ）を迎えるもので、海上では宗像三女神それぞれの御輿を載せた3隻の御座船（ござぶね）が宗像七浦（ななうら）の船団に供奉（ぐぶ）されて、海上を巡行する。[[漁船]]およそ数百隻による大規模な海上神幸行事で知られる。[[宗像氏]]は有史以前の上代、古代から[[海人族]](あまぞく)として知られ、優れた航海技術をもち、日本と大陸との交易に重要な役割を果たしてきたとされる。
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|海上神幸行事のシーン（2014年）]]
== メディアへの登場 == [[NHK福岡放送局]]制作の[[2003年]]の[[福岡発地域ドラマ]]「玄海〜わたしの海へ〜」のエンディングで用いられた。 [[2005年]]に[[RKB毎日放送]]制作の『[[ダイドードリンコ]]スペシャル 海と女神の物語 みあれ祭』で紹介された。
== 関連項目 == * [[宗像大社]] * [[船渡御]] * [[放生会]] * [[海人族]]
== 参考文献 == * 福岡市 編『ふくおか歴史散歩』（[[平野国臣]]が宗像大社の営繕を担当） * [[藤原新也]]・[[安部龍太郎]]『神の島 沖ノ島』（[[小学館]] 2013年）
{{event-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:みあれさい}} [[Category:福岡県の祭り]] [[Category:宗像市]] [[Category:10月]]

{{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}} as part of the {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in {{ill|Munakata City|ja|宗像市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島}}, each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御}} * {{ill|Hōjō-e|ja|放生会}} * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * {{ill|Shinya Fujiwara|ja|藤原新也}} and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.
{{event-stub}}

{{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}} as part of the {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in {{ill|Munakata City|ja|宗像市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島}}, each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御}} * {{ill|Hōjō-e|ja|放生会}} * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * {{ill|Shinya Fujiwara|ja|藤原新也}} and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.

revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}} as part of the {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in {{ill|Munakata City|ja|宗像市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島}}, each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御}} * {{ill|Hōjō-e|ja|放生会}} * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * {{ill|Shinya Fujiwara|ja|藤原新也}} and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.
{{event-stub}}

{{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}} as part of the {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in {{ill|Munakata City|ja|宗像市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島}}, each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御}} * {{ill|Hōjō-e|ja|放生会}} * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * {{ill|Shinya Fujiwara|ja|藤原新也}} and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.

The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御}} * {{ill|Hōjō-e|ja|放生会}} * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * {{ill|Shinya Fujiwara|ja|藤原新也}} and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.
{{event-stub}}
