{{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}} as part of the {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in {{ill|Munakata City|ja|宗像市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島}}, each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御}} * {{ill|Hōjō-e|ja|放生会}} * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * {{ill|Shinya Fujiwara|ja|藤原新也}} and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.
{{event-stub}}

revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}} as part of the {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in {{ill|Munakata City|ja|宗像市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島}}, each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御}} * {{ill|Hōjō-e|ja|放生会}} * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * {{ill|Shinya Fujiwara|ja|藤原新也}} and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.
{{event-stub}}

{{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}} as part of the {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in {{ill|Munakata City|ja|宗像市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島}}, each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御}} * {{ill|Hōjō-e|ja|放生会}} * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * {{ill|Shinya Fujiwara|ja|藤原新也}} and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.
{{event-stub}}

{{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}} as part of the {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in {{ill|Munakata City|ja|宗像市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島}}, each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.== See Also == * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御}} * {{ill|Hōjō-e|ja|放生会}} * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * {{ill|Shinya Fujiwara|ja|藤原新也}} and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.

revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}} as part of the {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in {{ill|Munakata City|ja|宗像市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島}}, each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.== See Also == * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御}} * {{ill|Hōjō-e|ja|放生会}} * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * {{ill|Shinya Fujiwara|ja|藤原新也}} and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.
{{event-stub}}

revision.comment: Bot: Reformatted page - moved categories, interwikis, DEFAULTSORT, and removed duplicates.
revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}} as part of the {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in {{ill|Munakata City|ja|宗像市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島}}, each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ}}.== See Also == * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御}} * {{ill|Hōjō-e|ja|放生会}} * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * {{ill|Shinya Fujiwara|ja|藤原新也}} and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.
{{event-stub}}

revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日|12=draft}} as part of the {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社|12=draft}} Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in {{ill|Munakata City|ja|宗像市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県|12=draft}}. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)|12=draft}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島|12=draft}}, each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|12=draft}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市杵島姫神|12=draft}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神|12=draft}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神|12=draft}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ|12=draft}}. == See Also == * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社|12=draft}} * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御|12=draft}} * {{ill|Hōjō-e|ja|放生会|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族|12=draft}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * {{ill|Shinya Fujiwara|ja|藤原新也|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎|12=draft}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2014年3月29日 (土) 09:58 (UTC)}} {{画像提供依頼|date=2014年3月29日 (土) 09:58 (UTC)|cat=宗像市|祭りの様子がわかる画像}} '''みあれ祭'''（みあれさい）は、毎年[[10月1日]]に[[福岡県]][[宗像市]]の[[宗像大社]]秋季大祭（田島放生会）の最初に行われる海上、航海安全や大漁などを願って行われる祭礼である。
== 内容 == 宗像大社は本土にある'''辺津宮'''、[[大島 (福岡県)|大島]]にある'''中津宮'''、[[沖ノ島]]にある'''沖津宮'''からなり、それぞれ姉妹神である[[宗像三女神]]が祀られている。みあれ祭は辺津宮に祀られている市杵島姫神（いちきしまひめ）が、姉にあたる田心姫神（たごりひめ）と湍津姫神（たぎつひめ）を迎えるもので、海上では宗像三女神それぞれの御輿を載せた3隻の御座船（ござぶね）が宗像七浦（ななうら）の船団に供奉（ぐぶ）されて、海上を巡行する。[[漁船]]およそ数百隻による大規模な海上神幸行事で知られる。[[宗像氏]]は有史以前の上代、古代から[[海人族]](あまぞく)として知られ、優れた航海技術をもち、日本と大陸との交易に重要な役割を果たしてきたとされる。
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|海上神幸行事のシーン（2014年）]]
== メディアへの登場 == [[NHK福岡放送局]]制作の[[2003年]]の[[福岡発地域ドラマ]]「玄海〜わたしの海へ〜」のエンディングで用いられた。 [[2005年]]に[[RKB毎日放送]]制作の『[[ダイドードリンコ]]スペシャル 海と女神の物語 みあれ祭』で紹介された。
== 関連項目 == * [[宗像大社]] * [[船渡御]] * [[放生会]] * [[海人族]]
== 参考文献 == * 福岡市 編『ふくおか歴史散歩』（[[平野国臣]]が宗像大社の営繕を担当） * [[藤原新也]]・[[安部龍太郎]]『神の島 沖ノ島』（[[小学館]] 2013年）
{{event-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:みあれさい}} [[Category:福岡県の祭り]] [[Category:宗像市]] [[Category:10月]]

The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.

== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōshima|qq=}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on [[:en:Okinoshima (Fukuoka)|Okinoshima]], each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Female Deities of Munakata|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市杵島姫神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タギツヒメ}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/DyDo Drinco|qq=}}. == See Also == * [[:en:Munakata Taisha|Munakata Taisha]] * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Boat Procession|qq=}} * [[:en:Life release|Hōjō-e]] * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ama People|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * [[:en:Shin'ya Fujiwara|Shinya Fujiwara]] and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūtarō Abe|qq=}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.
{{event-stub}}

During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市杵島姫神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タギツヒメ}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/DyDo Drinco|qq=|1=Dydo Drinco|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11316814}}. == See Also == * [[:en:Munakata Taisha|Munakata Taisha]] * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Boat Procession|qq=|1=funatogyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614282}} * [[:en:Life release|Hōjō-e]] * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ama People|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * [[:en:Shin'ya Fujiwara|Shinya Fujiwara]] and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūtarō Abe|qq=|1=Ryūtarō Abe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4721954}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:みあれ祭]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11278738]] Wikidata name: Miare Festival [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on [[:en:October 1|October 1]] as part of the [[:en:Munakata Taisha|Munakata Taisha]] Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in [[:en:Munakata, Fukuoka|Munakata City]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]]. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōshima|qq=}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on [[:en:Okinoshima (Fukuoka)|Okinoshima]], each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=Three Munakata Goddesses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Female Deities of Munakata|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市杵島姫神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タギツヒメ}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/DyDo Drinco|1=Dydo Drinco|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11316814}}. == See Also == * [[:en:Munakata Taisha|Munakata Taisha]] * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Boat Procession|1=funatogyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614282}} * [[:en:Life release|Hōjō-e]] * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ama People|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * [[:en:Shin'ya Fujiwara|Shinya Fujiwara]] and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūtarō Abe|1=Ryūtarō Abe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4721954}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.
{{event-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|みあれ祭|version=104193525|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:みあれ祭]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11278738]] Wikidata name: Miare Festival [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on [[:en:October 1|October 1]] as part of the [[:en:Munakata Taisha|Munakata Taisha]] Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in [[:en:Munakata, Fukuoka|Munakata City]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]]. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōshima|qq=}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on [[:en:Okinoshima (Fukuoka)|Okinoshima]], each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=Three Munakata Goddesses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Female Deities of Munakata|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市杵島姫神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タギツヒメ}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/DyDo Drinco|1=Dydo Drinco|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11316814}}. == See Also == * [[:en:Munakata Taisha|Munakata Taisha]] * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Boat Procession|1=funatogyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614282}} * [[:en:Life release|Hōjō-e]] * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ama People|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * [[:en:Shin'ya Fujiwara|Shinya Fujiwara]] and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūtarō Abe|1=Ryūtarō Abe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4721954}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:みあれ祭]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11278738]] Wikidata name: Miare Festival [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on [[:en:October 1|October 1]] as part of the [[:en:Munakata Taisha|Munakata Taisha]] Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in [[:en:Munakata, Fukuoka|Munakata City]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]]. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōshima|qq=}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on [[:en:Okinoshima (Fukuoka)|Okinoshima]], each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=Three Munakata Goddesses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Female Deities of Munakata|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市杵島姫神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タギツヒメ}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/DyDo Drinco|1=Dydo Drinco|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11316814}}. == See Also == * [[:en:Munakata Taisha|Munakata Taisha]] * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Boat Procession|1=funatogyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614282}} * [[:en:Life release|Hōjō-e]] * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ama People|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * [[:en:Shin'ya Fujiwara|Shinya Fujiwara]] and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūtarō Abe|1=Ryūtarō Abe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4721954}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.
{{event-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|みあれ祭|version=104193525|comment=Imported full ja history}}

== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/DyDo Drinco|1=Dydo Drinco|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11316814}}. == See Also == * [[:en:Munakata Taisha|Munakata Taisha]] * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Boat Procession|1=funatogyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614282}} * [[:en:Life release|Hōjō-e]] * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ama People|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:みあれ祭]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11278738]] Wikidata name: Miare Festival [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on [[:en:October 1|October 1]] as part of the [[:en:Munakata Taisha|Munakata Taisha]] Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in [[:en:Munakata, Fukuoka|Munakata City]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]]. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōshima|qq=}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on [[:en:Okinoshima (Fukuoka)|Okinoshima]], each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=Three Munakata Goddesses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Female Deities of Munakata|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市杵島姫神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タギツヒメ}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/DyDo Drinco|1=Dydo Drinco|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11316814}}. == See Also == * [[:en:Munakata Taisha|Munakata Taisha]] * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Boat Procession|1=funatogyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614282}} * [[:en:Life release|Hōjō-e]] * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ama People|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * [[:en:Shin'ya Fujiwara|Shinya Fujiwara]] and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūtarō Abe|1=Ryūtarō Abe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4721954}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.

In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/DyDo Drinco|1=Dydo Drinco|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11316814}}. == See Also == * [[:en:Munakata Taisha|Munakata Taisha]] * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Boat Procession|1=funatogyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614282}} * [[:en:Life release|Hōjō-e]] * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ama People|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}

During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市杵島姫神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タギツヒメ}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/DyDo Drinco|1=Dydo Drinco|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11316814}}. == See Also == * [[:en:Munakata Taisha|Munakata Taisha]] * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Boat Procession|1=funatogyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614282}} * [[:en:Life release|Hōjō-e]] * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ama People|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * [[:en:Shin'ya Fujiwara|Shinya Fujiwara]] and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūtarō Abe|1=Ryūtarō Abe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4721954}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:みあれ祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:みあれ祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:みあれ祭]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11278738]] Wikidata name: Miare Festival [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on [[:en:October 1|October 1]] as part of the [[:en:Munakata Taisha|Munakata Taisha]] Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in [[:en:Munakata, Fukuoka|Munakata City]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]]. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōshima|qq=}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on [[:en:Okinoshima (Fukuoka)|Okinoshima]], each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=Three Munakata Goddesses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Female Deities of Munakata|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市杵島姫神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タギツヒメ}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/DyDo Drinco|1=Dydo Drinco|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11316814}}. == See Also == * [[:en:Munakata Taisha|Munakata Taisha]] * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Boat Procession|1=funatogyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614282}} * [[:en:Life release|Hōjō-e]] * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ama People|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * [[:en:Shin'ya Fujiwara|Shinya Fujiwara]] and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūtarō Abe|1=Ryūtarō Abe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4721954}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.
{{event-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|みあれ祭|version=104193525|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:みあれ祭]]

In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/DyDo Drinco|1=Dydo Drinco|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11316814}}. == See Also == * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|en|Munakata Taisha|WD=Q704962|lt=Munakata Taisha}} * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Boat Procession|1=funatogyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614282}} * {{ill|Life release|en|Life release|WD=Q11078912|lt=Hōjō-e}} * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ama People|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:みあれ祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:みあれ祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:みあれ祭]]
{{Citation needed|date=March 29, 2014}} {{Image request|date=March 29, 2014|cat=Munakata City|image of the festival}} '''Miare Festival''' (みあれ祭, Miare-sai) is a religious festival held annually on {{ill|October 1|en|October 1|WD=Q2913|lt=October 1}} as part of the {{ill|Munakata Taisha|en|Munakata Taisha|WD=Q704962|lt=Munakata Taisha}} Autumn Grand Festival (Tajima Hōjō-e) in {{ill|Munakata, Fukuoka|en|Munakata, Fukuoka|WD=Q819603|lt=Munakata City}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture}}. The festival seeks blessings for maritime safety, successful voyages, and abundant catches.
== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōshima|qq=}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on {{ill|Okinoshima (Fukuoka)|en|Okinoshima (Fukuoka)|WD=Q7082047|lt=Okinoshima}}, each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=Three Munakata Goddesses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Female Deities of Munakata|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市杵島姫神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タギツヒメ}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/DyDo Drinco|1=Dydo Drinco|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11316814}}. == See Also == * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|en|Munakata Taisha|WD=Q704962|lt=Munakata Taisha}} * {{ill|Boat Procession|ja|船渡御|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Boat Procession|1=funatogyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614282}} * {{ill|Life release|en|Life release|WD=Q11078912|lt=Hōjō-e}} * {{ill|Ama People|ja|海人族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ama People|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}
== References == * ''Fukuoka Rekishi Sanpo'' (Historical Stroll through Fukuoka), edited by Fukuoka City (noting Hirano Kuninomi’s involvement in Munakata Taisha renovations). * {{ill|Shin'ya Fujiwara|en|Shin'ya Fujiwara|WD=Q5157839|lt=Shinya Fujiwara}} and {{ill|Ryūtarō Abe|ja|安部龍太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūtarō Abe|1=Ryūtarō Abe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4721954}}, ''Kami no Shima Okinoshima'' (God’s Island Okinoshima), Shogakukan, 2013.

== Details == Munakata Taisha comprises three shrines: '''Hetsu-miya''' on the mainland, '''Nakatsu-miya''' on {{ill|Ōshima|ja|大島 (福岡県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōshima|qq=}}, and '''Okitsu-miya''' on {{ill|Okinoshima (Fukuoka)|en|Okinoshima (Fukuoka)|WD=Q7082047|lt=Okinoshima}}, each dedicated to one of the {{ill|Three Female Deities of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=Three Munakata Goddesses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Female Deities of Munakata|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=}}. During the Miare Festival, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市杵島姫神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, enshrined at Hetsu-miya, symbolically welcomes her elder sisters, {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|田心姫神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ}} and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タギツヒメ}}.
The event involves a maritime procession where three portable shrines (mikoshi), representing the Munakata Three Goddesses, are placed on ceremonial boats (gozabune). These boats are escorted by a fleet from the Seven Harbors of Munakata (Nanoura) in a large-scale sea parade that includes hundreds of fishing boats. The event highlights the region's ancient seafaring heritage, as the Munakata clan, renowned from prehistoric to ancient times, played a pivotal role in trade and navigation between Japan and the mainland.
[[File:宗像大社みあれ祭.jpg|thumb|Scene from the maritime procession (2014)]]
== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/DyDo Drinco|1=Dydo Drinco|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11316814}}.

== Media Appearances == The festival was featured in the ending scene of the 2003 NHK Fukuoka regional drama ''Genkai: Watashi no Umi e'' (玄海〜わたしの海へ〜). In 2005, it was highlighted in the RKB Mainichi Broadcasting special ''The Story of the Sea and the Goddess: Miare Festival,'' sponsored by {{ill|DyDo Drinco|ja|ダイドードリンコ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/DyDo Drinco|1=Dydo Drinco|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11316814}}.
